coredata.dc:title: Mesoporous structured MIPs@CDs fluorescence sensor for highly sensitive detection of TNT
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Shoufang
Lu, Hongzhi
coredata.dc:description: A facile strategy was developed to prepare mesoporous structured molecularly imprinted polymers capped carbon dots (M-MIPs@CDs) fluorescence sensor for highly sensitive and selective determination of TNT. The strategy using amino-CDs directly as “functional monomer” for imprinting simplify the imprinting process and provide well recognition sites accessibility. The as-prepared M-MIPs@CDs sensor, using periodic mesoporous silica as imprinting matrix, and amino-CDs directly as “functional monomer”, exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity toward TNT with detection limit of 17nM. The recycling process was sustainable for 10 times without obvious efficiency decrease. The feasibility of the developed method in real samples was successfully evaluated through the analysis of TNT in soil and water samples with satisfactory recoveries of 88.6–95.7%. The method proposed in this work was proved to be a convenient and practical way to prepare high sensitive and selective fluorescence MIPs@CDs sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted polymers
Carbon dots
Mesoporous silica
TNT

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of mesoporous structured TNT imprinted fluorescence sensor
Synthesis of mesoporous structured MIPs@CDs fluorescence sensor
Molecular selectivity and sensing specificity of the sensor
Practical application to real sample analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thanks National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21307052 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China ( ZR2013BL006 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for preparation of mesoporous structured MIPs@CDs fluorescence probe.
TEM and SEM images of M-MIPs@CDs and M-MIPs, and TEM image of M-NIPs@CDs; N2 adsorption isotherms and the pore size distribution for M-MIPs@CDs.

The fluorescence spectra M-MIPs@CDs, MIPs@CDs, M-MIPs@CDs-APTES and M-NIPs@CDs upon the exposure to different concentrations of TNT. The experimental conditions were M-MIPs@CDs or M-NIPs@CDs 40μg/mL; excited light, 365nm; silt widths of excitation and emission, 3 and 3nm, respectively.

The selectivity of M-MIPs@CDs and M-NIPs@CDs to other template analogues and metal ions. The experimental conditions were M-MIPs@CDs or M-NIPs@CDs, 40μg/mL; excited light, 365nm; silt widths of excitation and emission, 3 and 3nm, respectively. The concentration of metal ions fixed as 10μM while the value was 0.5μM for template analogues.
Spiked recoveries and relative standard deviations for detection of TNT in spiked soil and tap water samples by M-MIPs@CDs.
Result±RSD% of the analysis of positive tap and soil samples using M-MIPs@CDs and National Standard Method.

head.ce:title: Mesoporous structured MIPs@CDs fluorescence sensor for highly sensitive detection of TNT

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Linyi University, Linyi 276005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Linyi University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile strategy was developed to prepare mesoporous structured molecularly imprinted polymers capped carbon dots (M-MIPs@CDs) fluorescence sensor for highly sensitive and selective determination of TNT. The strategy using amino-CDs directly as “functional monomer” for imprinting simplify the imprinting process and provide well recognition sites accessibility. The as-prepared M-MIPs@CDs sensor, using periodic mesoporous silica as imprinting matrix, and amino-CDs directly as “functional monomer”, exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity toward TNT with detection limit of 17nM. The recycling process was sustainable for 10 times without obvious efficiency decrease. The feasibility of the developed method in real samples was successfully evaluated through the analysis of TNT in soil and water samples with satisfactory recoveries of 88.6–95.7%. The method proposed in this work was proved to be a convenient and practical way to prepare high sensitive and selective fluorescence MIPs@CDs sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are a class of polymers which have specific recognition sites complementary to template molecules (Chen et al., 2011). Fluorescence sensors displayed widely application in trace amounts of analytes detection because of high sensitivity, simplicity and cost-effective, (Lee et al., 2015). Fluorescence MIPs sensors, which combined the high sensitivity of fluorescence sensor with the high selectivity of MIPs, displayed apparent advantages when detection trace substances in complicated matrix (Chen et al., 2016). Up to date, two methods were widely used to prepare MIPs fluorescence sensors. One was using fluorescence functional monomer for molecular imprinting. For example, naphthalimide-based monomer (Wagner et al., 2013) or dansyl methacrylate (Inoue et al., 2013) were synthesized for molecular imprinting. This method was involved in complicated functional monomer synthesis procedure. The other method was encapsulating fluorescent nanoparticles, such as semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) (Chantada-Vázquez et al., 2016), nanocluster (Wu et al., 2015) or upconversion nanoparticles (Liu et al., 2014) into MIPs. Comparing to the first method, this method of direct doping fluorescent nanoparticles enjoys the advantages of simple procedure. Due to the high emission quantum yields and size tunable emission property, QDs have become one of the most extensively optical sensing nanomaterials for molecular imprinting. For example, MIPs@QDs sensors have been designed for detection of cytochrome c (Zhang et al., 2011b, 2011a), glycoproteins (Zhang et al., 2014) and TNT (Xu et al., 2013; Stringer et al., 2010), etc.

However, QDs are associated with inherent heavy metal toxicity, and noble metal clusters involve high cost. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have gained tremendous attention for their unique properties, such as green synthesis, excellent solubility, easy functionalization, and good biocompability, etc. As a green substitute for QDs, CDs have found more promising applications in fluorescence sensors (Lin et al., 2011; Yang et al., 2015; Barati et al., 2015; Dong et al., 2012). While CDs emerge as superior and universal fluorophores, very limited studies were carried out to use the CDs as optical probe for molecular imprinting (Wang et al., 2015a, 2015b). The phenomena maybe explained as follows. Some CDs sensors were based on fact that aggregation of CDs would resulted in the fluorescence quenching of CDs. (Shen and Xia, 2014). However, this mechanism was not an ideal way to design MIPs sensors.

As a powerful explosive compounds, trinitrotoluene (TNT) has raised worldwide concerns regarding public security and environmental problems. Moreover, TNT is toxic to creatures. Exposure to TNT may cause pancytopenia, a disorder of the blood-forming tissues in humans and other mammals (Bradley and Chapelle, 1995). Thus, developing practicable analytical platforms to monitor TNT is urgent and necessary.

Considering the above, we want to propose a facile strategy to immobilize fluorescent CDs into MIPs for highly sensitive and selective TNT detection. However, compared to fluorescence nanoparticles sensors, such as CDs sensors or QDs sensors, the sensitivity of MIPs fluorescence sensors often were weakened (Xu et al., 2013). The reason would be the following. First, It is hard to embedded fluorescence nanoparticles uniformly into highly cross-linked MIPs, so it is difficult to guarantee that each imprinted cavity was surrounded by fluorescent nanoparticles. Second, the sphere or irregular bulk MIPs particles was not beneficial for mass transfer. Many strategies have been proposed to solve the above mentioned problems. For example, Zhang's group (Li et al., 2013) immobilized QDs on the surface of silica nanoparticles by covalently linking carboxyl-terminated CdTe QDs and amino functionalized silica nanoparticles to provide better recognition site accessibility. From the point of molecular imprinting and fluorescence quenching mechanism, using fluorescent nanoparticles directly as “functional monomer” would be an idea way to guarantee that each imprinted cavity was surrounded by fluorescent nanoparticles, further to improve the sensitivity of fluorescent sensor. Dong's group (Zhang et al., 2015) reported that N-rich CDs without further modification can be applied as sensing platform for detection of TNT based on the fact that strong TNT–amino interaction can quench the fluorescent of amino-CDs through charge transfer. So amino-CDs could be used as “functional monomer” for TNT sensing.

Previous work have proved that hierarchical architectures, such as mesoporous materials (Tan et al., 2009; Xu et al., 2015), inverted opal structures (Fang et al., 2009), can enhance the sensitivity of fluorescence sensor. In our previous work, mesoporous structured MIPs@QDs sensors for TNT (Xu and Lu, 2015b) and melamine (Xu and Lu, 2015a) were prepared, and the results indicated that mesoporous structures can greatly decrease mass-transport resistance and provide easier accessibility to target species. These features make mesoporous silica particularly suitable for imprinting.

Herein, we report a facile strategy to immobilize amino-CDs into mesoporous structured imprinted polymers for highly sensitive and selective detection of TNT. The selectivity was guaranteed by molecular imprinting technique while the sensitivity was improved by two methods. One was using mesoporous silica as imprinting matrix, and the other was using fluorescent amino-CDs directly as “functional monomer”. Mesoporous structured MIPs@CDs (M-MIPs@CDs) were prepared using amino-CDs as functional monomer, trinitrophenol (TNP) as dummy template. Condensation of tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS) in the presence of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), amino-CDs and TNP, can form M-MIPs@CDs. Compared to M-MIPs@CDs-APTES which using 3-aminopropyl triethoxy silane (APTES) as functional monomer, the sensitivity of as-prepared M-MIPs@CDs was significantly enhanced.

Citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA), trinitrophenol (TNP), dinitrotoluene (DNT), 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were purchased from Tianjin Reagent Plant (Tianjin, China). TNT standard solution (1.00mg/mL in methanol), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and 3-aminopropyl triethoxy silane (APTES) were purchased from J&K Technology Ltd. (Beijng, China). TNT standard solution was diluted with ultrapure water for fluorescent detection.

A Fluoromax-4 Spectrofluorometer (Horiba Scientific) was used for fluorescence measurements. A transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F) and a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800 FE–SEM) were used for the morphological evaluation. A Micromeritics ASAP 2020 Sorptometer (Micromeritics, ASAP 2020, USA) was used for determination of Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area. A Thermo Scientific NanoDrop 2000/2000c spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA) was employed for UV–vis spectra recording. PerkinElmer PE-983 FT-IR spectro-photometer was used for infrared spectra detection.

In a typical way, 1.0g CA and 5.0mL EDA was dissolved in 25mL deionized water. The mixed solution was then transferred into a 50mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. The precursor solution was heated to and maintained at 200°C for 5h. After cooled to room temperature, the resulted amino-CDs were dialyzed for 1 days (molecular porous membrane tubing, cutoff 1000) at room temperature to remove the unreacted moieties. For CDs without amino functionalization, only CA was used as precursor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of mesoporous structured TNT imprinted fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Mesoporous structured TNT imprinted fluorescence CDs sensors (M-MIPs@CDs) were prepared using TNP as dummy template as reported in our previous work (Xu et al., 2013). Typically, 2mL amino-CDs and 10mg TNP were mixed and allowed to reaction for 1h to form amino-CDs-TNP complex. Then 0.7mL NaOH (2.0M) was added to 100mL of CTAB solution (5.5×10−3 M). The mixture was heated to 70°C, followed by the addition of 1.0mL TEOS, amino-CDs-TNP complex. The resulting mixture was stirred at 70°C for 3h before cooled to room temperature. The precipitate was collected and washed with water and ethanol several times. The control non-imprinted fluorescence sensor (M-NIPs@CDs) was prepared as described above but without template TNP. The control M-MIPs@CDs-APTES was prepared as described above but using APTES as functional monomer, CDs only as fluorescence nanoparticles. The control non mesoporous imprinted fluorescence sensor (MIPs@CDs) was prepared as described above but without template CTAB. Templates TNP for molecular recognition and template CTAB for mesoporous structure were removed by washing repeatedly with a mixture of water/ethanol under ultrasound assistance (Xu et al., 2015).

The binding capacity of M-MIPs@CDs and M-NIPs@CDs was evaluated by adsorption experiment. Typically, 30mg M-MIPs@CDs or M-NIPs@CDs were added to 3.0mL of 20mg/L or 40mg/L TNT solution, and placed on the oscillator at room temperature for 2h. Then the supernatant were separated by centrifugation and detected by HPLC.

The TNT detection procedure was carried out in ultrapure water. In a typical run, TNT was added into 4.0mL of 40μg/mL probe solution one by one with the final concentrations of 50nM to 2.5μM. The fluorescence spectra were collected 10min after each addition under excitation at 365nm with slit widths setting at 3/3nm.

The removal-rebinding cycles were repeated 10 times using the same M-MIPs@CDs. During the process, M-MIPs@CDs could be recovered by washing with ethanol/water solvent to remove TNT. The reusable experiments were carried out in successive batches. In the first cycle, the florescence detection was conducted within 10min. After recovered, M-MIPs@CDs particles were poured into the refreshed TNT solution for the second cycle detection. This process was repeated for 10 times while quenching efficiency was recorded.

In order to test of selectivity of M-MIPs@CDs, the fluorescent responses to other template analogues (DNP, 4-NP and DNT), competitive molecular (tartrazine) and metal ions (Cu2+, Hg2+) were examined by the similar procedure mentioned above. The concentration of template analogues were fixed as 0.5μM. During competitive experiment, TNT was fixed at 0.5μM, and increase the ratio of tartrazine to TNT from 0.5 to 3.0 by changing the concentration of tartrazine.

TNT was first retrieved from soil samples by acetone, then acetone was evaporated with the help of nitrogen followed by dissolution in water. Tap water sample which acquired in the laboratory was filtered through 0.45µm membrane to remove suspended particles. After addition of TNT, the fluorescence intensity of M-MIPs@CDs was recorded. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained by repeating the experiment 3 times under the same condition.

Furthermore, in order to demonstrate the suitability of this method for analysis of real-life samples, M-MIPs@CDs were applied to test incurred samples contaminated with TNT. The water and soil samples contaminated with TNT ranging from 0.5 to 1.0μM were provided by Food Quality Supervision Bureau of Linyi. The samples were analyzed using M-MIPs@CDs and the results were compared with those obtained using the National Standard Method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of mesoporous structured MIPs@CDs fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Over the past years, several methods have been developed for synthesis of CDs, such as electrochemical oxidation (Zheng et al., 2009) and microwave irradiation (Gong et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2015a, 2015b; Zhang et al., 2016). However, hydrothermal carbonization (Moon et al., 2016; Dhenadhayalan et al., 2016) has displayed great advancement due to its simplicity and high quantum yield. So in the present work, CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. Some green synthesis methods using aloe (Xu et al., 2015), milk (Wang and Zhou, 2014) or garlic (Zhao et al., 2015) as carbon source have been developed via hydrothermal process. However, the fluorescence quantum yields were relatively lower when compared to CDs prepared by CA. So in this work, amino-CDs were prepared using CA as carbon resource and EDA as nitrogen resource. TEM image of amino-CDs was shown in Fig. S1. The amino-CDs were nearly spherical and have a narrow size range of 5–7nm. The existence of amino groups were confirmed by FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1). The absorption at 1350cm−1 was from the stretching vibration of C-N and the peaks at 1600cm−1 related to the N-H bend vibrations. The peaks indicated the successful introduction of nitrogen atoms into the CDs. It should be pointed out the amount of EDA has significant impact on the fluorescence intensity and quenching efficiency (Fig. S2).

APTES was often used as functional monomer for TNT imprinting based on the fact that strong acid−base pairing interaction existed between the electron-rich amino groups and electron efficient nitroaromatic rings. However, fluorescence nanoparticles were hard to dope into highly cross-linked MIPs uniformly, so it is hard to guarantee that each imprinted cavity was surrounded by fluorescent nanoparticles, which would resulted in the relative lower quenching efficiency. In the present work, amino-CDs was directly used as functional monomer, which provided better recognition site accessibility (Fig. S3).

TNT involves safety concerns and cannot be purchased easily. Accordingly, dummy molecular imprinting technique was adopted. In our previous work (Xu et al., 2013), we have proved that TNP, with similar shape and size to TNT, could be used as dummy template for TNT recognition. So in this work, TNP was used as dummy template for imprinting.

The whole synthesis procedure for M-MIPs@CDs were showed in Fig. 1. First, amino-CDs were prepared using CA and EDA as precursor by hydrothermal method. Then amino-CDs and template TNP were mixed to form template-functional monomer complex by acid–base pairing interaction. Subsequently, TNT imprinted MIPs@CDs was prepared by condensation TEOS in the presence of amino-CDs-TNP complex. To form mesoporous structure, CTAB was used as soft template. After removal of CTAB and TNP, M-MIPs@CDs for recognition of TNT were obtained.

The fluorescence spectrum of amino-CDs and M-MIPs@CDs probe were characterized (Fig. S4). Amino-CDs showed maximum fluorescence emission at 443nm and exhibited strong blue fluorescence light when excited by 365nm UV light. The fluorescence spectrum of M-MIPs@CDs probes showed a slight red shift while still displayed blue fluorescence light. The fluorescence stability of M-MIPs@CDs was evaluated by repeated detection of the fluorescence intensity every day. After stored for 10 days, the fluorescence intensity of M-MIPs@CDs did not showed significant decrease (Fig. S6). This result implied that the as-prepared M-MIPs@CDs fluorescence sensors have acceptable storage stability.

The morphology of M-MIPs@CDs and M-NIPs@CDs were characterized by TEM. From Fig. 2 we can see that although particle size distribution is not highly uniform, M-MIPs@CDs and M-NIPs@CDs have the similar diameter and morphology. When compared M-MIPs without CDs, we can see that the existence of CDs have influence on the morphology and diameter of mesoporous silica. The worm-like or hexagonal tunnels channels were clearly present in M-MIPs without CDs, which suggest the existence of mesoporous structure. However, with the addition of CDs, mesoporous channel become blurred. In the meanwhile, some black dots (CDs) can be observed in M-MIPs@CDs. With the addition of CDs, the diameter of M-MIPs decreased from 150nm to 80nm. In order to further confirm the existence of CDs in M-MIPs, SEM images were provided. From Fig. 2 we can see that no significant difference could be observed between the surface of M-MIPs@CDs and M-MIPs. So we can draw the conclusion that CDs were doped into M-MIPs, not package on the surface of M-MIPs. In addition, the porous structure of M-MIPs@CDs can be confirmed by Nitrogen adsorption–desorption experiment, and the type IV isotherm curves confirmed the existence of mesoporous pores.

Binding property of M-MIPs@CDs were investigated by adsorption of template TNT using M-NIPs@CDs as control. The adsorption capacities were displayed in Fig. S5. The adsorption capacity of M-MIPs@CDs was 1.62mg/g while the initial TNT concentration was 20mg/L, which was much higher than M-NIPs@CDs of 0.21mg/g. When the initial concentration of TNT increased to 40mg/L, the binding capacity of M-MIPs@CDs and M-NIPs@CDs was 2.94mg/g and 0.32mg/g, respectively. The phenomenon can be explained by the special cavities of TNT formed during the imprinting process in M-MIPs@CDs. Imprinting factor a, defined as Q MIP/Q NIP was calculated to be 7.8 and 9.2, respectively. The high imprinting factors implied the high selectivity of MIPs.

The dose response of M-MIPs@CDs to TNT were examined. Firstly, variables such as amount of M-MIPs@CDs, dispersion medium and response time were optimized using quenching amount, defined as (F 0−F)/F 0, as the index. Finally, the concentration of M-MIPs@CDs was fixed at 40μg/mL, ultrapure water was chosen for quenching assays, and 10min was enough for response (Figs. S6). It should be pointed out that the quenching kinetics achieved a plateau within 4min. The rapid response speed can be attributed to the advantage of mesoporous structure and improved recognition sites accessibility.

Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity changes of M-MIPs@CDs to TNT was investigated. Upon the addition of TNT, the fluorescence intensity of M-MIPs@CDs at 445nm was continuously quenched. The quenching mechanism could be attributed to the electron transfer between amino group on CDs surface and TNT. The electron transfer from amino groups to aromatic rings leads to the formation of a Meisenheimer complex between TNT and the primary amino groups (Zhang et al., 2011a, 2015b). The feasibility of quenching-based TNT detection was illustrated by UV–vis adsorption. When adding TNT into EDA solution, a new visible absorbance from 460 to 520nm could be observed, and the color of the solution changed from colorless to light red, verifying the strong interaction between the TNT and EDA (Fig. S7A). The emission spectrum of CDs overlaps well with the absorption spectrum of the TNT–amine complex, which resulted in fluorescence quenching of CDs. Fluorescence response between CDs (amino-CDs and CDs) and TNT were estimated to further confirm the quenching mechanism and the results were displayed in Fig. S7B. With addition of TNT, the fluorescence intensity of CDs did not displayed significance change while amino-CDs was greatly quenched. So the amino group on the surface of CDs played important role for TNT detection.

Fig. 3 displayed the TNT concentration-dependent fluorescence change of M-MIPs@CDs. It was found that the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the continuous addition of TNT due to the continuous formation of TNT-amine complex when TNT enter the recognition sites. The fluorescence quenching amount was closely linear over the concentration of TNT ranging from 50nM to 2.0μM, which can be used for the quantification of TNT with the detection limit as low as 17nM (3S/N). The fluorescence quenching in this system followed the Stern-Volmer equation:

F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
s v
C
q
.

where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of M-MIPs@CDs in the absence and presence of TNT, respectively. The C q is the concentration of the TNT, K sv is the quenching constant. From Fig. 3(A) K sv, M-MIPs@CDs was calculated to be 0.94μM−1.

Meanwhile, the advantage of the using amino-CDs directly as functional monomer was verified by comparison with M-MIPs@CDs-APTES. As can be seen from Fig. 3 that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of M-MIPs@CDs-APTES was relatively lower than M-MIPs@CDs. Meanwhile, K sv, M-MIPs@CDs was 1.67 times higher K sv, M-MIPs@CDs-APTES. The comparison clearly showed that using amino-CDs as functional monomer provided well recognition accessibility and higher quenching efficiency. So M-MIPs@CDs possesses higher sensitivity than M-MIPs@CDs-APTES.

In order to verify the advantage of mesoporous structure, non-mesoporous structured fluorescence MIPs (MIPs@CDs) were prepared as control. The linear range 0.5–20μM and the LOD of 0.19μM were attained for MIPs@CDs. The sensitivity of MIPs@CDs was marked lower than mesoporous structured one. The results can be attributed to the mesoporous structure. The higher surface area and better accessibility for the binding sites are the reasons for the improved sensitivity.

The dose response of M-NIPs@CDs to TNT also has been examined. From Fig. 3 we can see that fluorescence intensity of M-NIPs@CDs also can be quenched by TNT. However, the sensitivity was much lower when compared to M-MIPs@CDs. M-NIPs@CDs displayed two linear rang form 50nM to 250nM and from 0.25μM to 2.5μM. This phenomenon can be explained as no recognition sites was existed in M-NIPs@CDs. Only those CDs located near the surface of M-NIPs@CDs can be quenched while most of CDs keep constant. So after the fluorescence intensity of the CDs located on the surface was quenched obviously, further increase of the amount of TNT given little help to quench CDs. The imprinting factor (IF), which is the ratio of K SV, MIP to K SV, NIP was used to evaluate the selectivity of the materials. Under optimum conditions, IF was calculated to be 2.32 and 25.7, respectively, indicating the better selectivity of M-MIPs@CDs.

The reusability of M-MIPs@CDs was evaluated by using it detection of 1.0μM TNT for 10 times. From Fig. S8 we can see that the sensor could retain its fluorescence intensity and detection sensitivity during those 10 recycles (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular selectivity and sensing specificity of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of M-MIPs@CDs were verified by competitive binding test. The quenching efficiency was recorded after addition of TNT and other template analogues (DNP, 4-NP and DNT), metal ions (Cu2+, Hg2+) or competitor (tartrazine). It can be seen from Fig. 4 that the quenching efficiency of M-MIPs@CDs solution was about 0.5 by 0.5μM TNT while no obvious quenching phenomenon could be observed after adding DNP, 4-NP or DNT in the probe solution. It has been reported that Cu2+ (Dong et al., 2012), Hg2+ (Lan et al., 2014) also can quenching CDs. However, in the TNT imprinted polymers, no selective recognition sites for metal ions were existed, so the quenching of Cu2+ and Hg2+ can be effectively prevented. Tartrazine (TAR) caused little fluorescence quenching when compared to TNT because the recognition sites of TNT imprinted cavities were not complementary to tartrazine, resulting in less chance to quench CDs.

Competitive binding experiments were also performed by varying the ratio of TAR to TNT. As shown in Fig. 4, when the concentration of TNT was fixed, the quenching amount displayed no significant change with the increase of the C TAR/C TNT ratio. Through the selectivity and competitive experiments, it can be confirmed that M-MIPs@CDs had specific recognition ability toward TNT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application to real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the practical application of M-MIPs@CDs, detection of TNT in soil and water samples was carried out. Spiking experiment was carried out because TNT did not be detected in tap water and soil sample. The estimated recoveries and RSD were shown in Table 1 and the results indicated that the recoveries of TNT for real samples were satisfied.

The method was further applied to test positive samples that were contaminated with TNT ranging from 0.5 to1.0μM, which were provided by Food Quality Supervision Bureau of Linyi. The samples were analyzed using M-MIPs@CDs and the results were compared with National Standard Method ( Table 2). From Table 2 we can see that the values in our method agree well with those obtained by accredited method. The results demonstrated that M-MIPs@CDs was an efficient and effective methodology for sensitive assessment of TNT at low concentration.

Much excellent work about chemo-sensors for TNT detection have been reported, and some were summarized in Table S1. From Table S1 we can see that many fluorescence nanoparticles, such as CDs (Zhang et al., 2015) and QDs (Gao et al., 2008) can be directly used to detect TNT with high sensitivity (we can call them CDs sensor or QDs sensor). However, the selectivity of those sensors was relatively low because some metal ions or other structure analogy also can quench those fluorescence nanoparticles. In order to improve the selectivity of those sensor, MIPs were introduced. However, the sensitivity of MIPs@CDs (or MIPs@QDs) sensors was lower than corresponding CDs sensors (Zhang et al., 2015) because of the highly cross-linked nature of MIPs. However, when we compared those MIPs fluorescence sensors, we can safely draw the conclusion that the sensitivity of MIPs fluorescence sensors can be improved by periodic mesoporous silica imprinting matrix. In our previous work we have reported that M-MIPs@QDs with LOD of 15nM for TNT (Xu et al., 2015). The LOD of 17nM of M-MIPs@CDs was not better than M-MIPs@QDs. The reasons may be the follows. In previous work, the sensitivity of M-MIPs@QDs was improved by two ways. One was ratiometric fluorescence technique, and the other was using periodic mesoporous silica as imprinting matrix. In the present work, the sensitivity of M-MIPs@CDs sensors was improved by periodic mesoporous silica and CDs directly used as functional monomers. So in our following work, ratiometric fluorescence technique would be adopted in order to further improve the sensitivity of M-MIPs@CDs sensors.

In summary, a novel strategy using amino-CDs directly as functional monomer for preparation of M-MIPs@CDs fluorescence probe was proposed, and its utility has been established for detection real samples. The sensitivity of M-MIPs@CDs was improved by using periodic mesoporous silica particles as imprinting matrix and using amino-CDs directly as “functional monomer”. Due to simplicity and effectivity, the strategy proposed in this work exhibits great promise as universe method for other pollutant analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thanks National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21307052), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China (ZR2013BL006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.06.020.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
